en_explanation: A special word that goes between a number and a noun in Chinese. You must use the correct measure word for each type of object, like 'three books' needs 本 (běn).
Another name for measure word. It classifies and counts nouns by grouping them into categories based on their shape or type.
The rise and fall of your voice pitch on a syllable in Chinese. There are four main tones, plus a neutral tone. Changing the tone changes the word's meaning completely.
zh_example_en: mother, hemp, horse, scold, question particle

en_explanation: When two tones come together, one tone changes to help you speak more naturally. For example, two third tones together become second tone then third tone.
zh_example_en: hello (two third tones, first becomes second)

en_explanation: A very light tone with no pitch change. It is less emphasized and sounds like you are simply saying the sound without worrying about going up or down.
The Romanization system for Chinese that uses Latin alphabet letters to show how to pronounce Chinese words. It includes tone marks above the vowels.
A single Chinese written symbol. Unlike letters in English, one character usually represents one complete syllable or morpheme with its own meaning.
The main building block of a Chinese character that gives you a clue about the word's meaning. Characters are organized in dictionaries by their radical.
The correct sequence of pen strokes to write a Chinese character. Following the proper stroke order makes your handwriting look correct and helps you learn characters better.
A modern Chinese character with fewer strokes, used mainly in mainland China and Singapore. These characters are easier to write than traditional ones.
The older, more complex form of Chinese characters with more strokes. These are mainly used in Taiwan, Hong Kong, and Chinese diaspora communities.
A word made of only one syllable or character. Most basic nouns and verbs in Chinese are monosyllabic, like 书 (book) or 走 (go).
A word made of two syllables or characters. Modern Chinese uses many disyllabic words instead of monosyllabic ones, like 学生 (student).
The particle 了 shows that an action is finished or completed. It does not give time, so you can use it with past, present, or future situations.
The particle 过 shows that you have done something at least one time before. It emphasizes your experience with the action, not when it happened.
The particle 着 shows that an action is happening right now or in progress. It describes a continuing state or action at a specific time.
The verb form that shows an action happening right now, at this moment. In Chinese, you make this with 正在, 在, or 着.
The verb form showing that you have had an experience with something before. In Chinese, you use 过 to mark this.
zh_example_en: I have watched this movie before.

en_explanation: The particle 吗 goes at the end of a statement to turn it into a yes-no question. It is the most common way to ask questions in Chinese.
The particle 呢 goes at the end to ask a question or show a contrasting situation. You use it when comparing or asking about something different.
The particle 吧 at the end softens a statement or suggests something. It can mean 'let's', 'I suppose', or 'what do you think?'
A small word at the end of a sentence that changes the mood or feeling. It doesn't change the basic meaning, but makes the sentence sound softer or more certain.
A verb that comes before the main verb to show ability, permission, or necessity. Examples are 能 (can), 会 (know how), 应该 (should), 要 (need to).
A word that comes after a verb to show the result or outcome of an action. It tells you what happened after the verb, like 做好 (do well) or 吃完 (finish eating).
A word that comes after a verb to show direction or position. It tells where something goes or where an action happens, like 进去 (go in) or 走出来 (come out).
A verb construction where the result of the action is directly shown in the verb phrase. It combines the main verb and a result word together, like 吃完 (finish eating).
A sentence structure where you first state the topic (what you are talking about), then give a comment about it. Chinese uses this more than English.
zh_example_pinyin: zhè běn shū, wǒ hěn xǐhuān.
zh_example_en: This book, I like it very much.

en_explanation: A sentence with two or more verbs in a row that show related or sequential actions. Each verb has its own object sometimes, like 买一本书给她 (buy a book for her).
When you repeat a word or part of a word twice. This can show a continuous action, an adjective that is softer, or create a new meaning.
The particle 的 shows possession or describes a noun. It connects an adjective or possession word to the noun it modifies, like 红的书 (red book).
A question word that asks for information. Examples are 什么 (what), 谁 (who), 哪里 (where), 为什么 (why), 怎样 (how).
The sequence or arrangement of words in a sentence. Chinese word order is quite strict and different from English, like subject-verb-object-location.
The first consonant sound or group of sounds at the start of a syllable in Chinese. Examples are b, p, m, f, d, t, n, l, g, k, h.
The vowel sound or vowel plus consonant sound that comes after the initial consonant in a syllable. Examples are a, an, ang, o, ong, e, en, eng, i, in, ing.
A sound unit in language that usually has one vowel. In Chinese, one syllable equals one character, and each syllable has an initial, a final, and a tone.
A word that names a person, place, thing, or idea. In Chinese, nouns do not change form for singular or plural like in English.
A word that shows an action or a state of being. Examples are 吃 (eat), 走 (walk), 是 (be), 有 (have). Verbs in Chinese do not change form for person or tense.
A word that describes or modifies a noun. In Chinese, adjectives usually come right before the noun and can sometimes act like verbs, like 很漂亮 (very pretty).
A word that modifies or describes a verb, adjective, or another adverb. It often shows how, when, where, or to what degree something happens, like 很 (very) or 快 (quickly).
The noun or pronoun at the beginning of a sentence that does the action or is being described. The subject usually comes before the verb in English and Chinese.
The verb or verb phrase in a sentence that shows what the subject does or its state. It tells what is happening or the quality of the subject.
The noun or pronoun that receives the action of the verb. The object usually comes after the verb in English and Chinese, like 书 in 我读书 (I read books).
The main noun that receives the action directly. It answers the question 'what' or 'whom'. In 我爱你, 你 is the direct object.
The noun that receives the result of the action indirectly. It answers the question 'to whom' or 'for whom', like 她 in 我给她一本书 (I give her a book).
A word or phrase that describes or modifies a noun, coming before it. In Chinese, adjectives and possessives are attributes, connected by 的.
Any word or phrase that works like an adjective to describe another word. In Chinese, attributives come before the noun they modify.
A word or phrase that modifies a verb, adjective, or sentence by giving information about how, when, where, or why. In Chinese, adverbials come before the verb.
zh_example_en: at school (adverbial in '我在学校读书')

en_explanation: A word or phrase that comes after a verb to complete its meaning or show its result, manner, or direction. It is different from an object because it adds to the verb, not the subject.
A group of words with a subject and verb that can stand alone or depend on another clause. Chinese sentences often have multiple clauses joined together.
zh_example: 当我到家的时候，妈妈在做饭。
zh_example_pinyin: dāng wǒ dàojiā de shíhou, māma zài zuòfàn.
zh_example_en: When I arrived home, mother was cooking.

en_explanation: A group of words that work together but do not have a complete subject and verb. Phrases can modify nouns, verbs, or adjectives.
A complete unit of language that has a subject and a predicate and can stand alone. A sentence expresses a complete thought or idea.
A small word that does not change form and has a grammar or emotion function. Particles often come at the end of a sentence to change its mood.
zh_example_en: How are you? (吗 is a question particle)

en_explanation: A small word that shows the relationship between a noun and other words in a sentence, like position, direction, or time. Examples are 在 (at), 从 (from), 到 (to).
A word that connects words, phrases, or clauses together. Examples are 和 (and), 或 (or), 但是 (but), 因为 (because).
A word that replaces a noun to avoid repeating it. Examples are 我 (I), 你 (you), 他 (he), 她 (she), 它 (it), 我们 (we), 他们 (they).
A structure where the subject and its predicate relationship forms the core of a sentence or clause. This is the basic sentence pattern in Chinese.
A word that shows quantity or amount before a noun. It can be a number like 三 (three) or a quantity word like 很多 (many), 一些 (some).
A word that makes a sentence negative or denies something. In Chinese, 不 (no/not) is the main negative marker for most verbs and adjectives.
A verb that links a subject to a noun, adjective, or other description. In Chinese, 是 (be) is the main copula, used to show identity or class.
The smallest unit of language that has meaning. In Chinese, most morphemes are single characters, and words can be made of one or more morphemes.
